attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man is reviewed on the ward after his morning blood tests showed a sodium of 126 mmol/L (135-145). He was admitted three days ago with worsening shortness of breath and peripheral oedema. He has a medical history of congestive heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, type 2 diabetes mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis, anxiety and hypertension. His medications include sertraline, furosemide, ramipril, metformin, carvedilol, prednisolone, methotrexate and folic acid.

On examination, he appears mildly short of breath at rest. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. He has bilateral pitting oedema to the mid-shin, raised jugular venous pressure, and bibasal chest crackles.

Investigations reveal:

  • Sodium 126 mmol/L (135-145)
  • Potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
  • Creatinine 95 µmol/L (45-90)
  • Urea 8.2 mmol/L (2.5-7.5)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of his hyponatraemia?
options: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hypervolaemic hyponatraemia in the context of decompensated heart failure, as evidenced by peripheral oedema, raised JVP, and bibasal crackles. The clinical features suggest fluid overload with dilutional hyponatraemia.

Fluid overload causing dilutional hyponatraemia is common in heart failure due to increased antidiuretic hormone (ADH) secretion triggered by reduced cardiac output. This leads to water retention out of proportion to sodium retention, resulting in a dilutional hyponatraemia. Importantly, this ADH secretion is physiologically appropriate as a response to perceived hypovolaemia from poor cardiac output, despite the patient being clinically hypervolaemic. This distinguishes it from syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH), where ADH release occurs without physiological stimulus. Furthermore, the ADH release will occur with activation of the renin angiotensin aldosterone system at large.

Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) typically presents with euvolaemic hyponatraemia without signs of fluid overload or depletion. Common causes include malignancy, central nervous system disorders, and certain medications.

Excessive diuretic use typically causes hypovolaemic hyponatraemia with signs of volume depletion such as postural hypotension and reduced skin turgor. This patient shows clear signs of fluid overload instead.

Hypothyroidism can cause hyponatraemia through decreased cardiac output and glomerular filtration rate, leading to impaired water excretion. However, severe hypothyroidism would typically present with additional features such as bradycardia, cold intolerance, and delayed reflexes, which are not described in this case.

Adrenal insufficiency presents with hypovolaemic hyponatraemia, often accompanied by hyperkalaemia, hypotension, and other signs of cortisol deficiency, which are not present in this case.

Management should focus on treating the underlying heart failure with careful diuresis and fluid restriction. Rapid correction of sodium should be avoided to prevent central pontine myelinolysis.
learningPoints: Hypervolaemic hyponatraemia typically occurs in conditions of fluid overload where sodium is diluted due to excess water retention.
Clinical assessment of volume status is crucial in determining the cause and management of hyponatraemia.
Treatment of hypervolaemic hyponatraemia focuses on managing the underlying condition and fluid restriction.
references: Rondon H, Badireddy M. Hyponatremia. [Updated 2023 Jun 14]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470386/
Dineen R, Thompson CJ, Sherlock M. Hyponatraemia - presentations and management. Clin Med (Lond). 2017 Jun;17(3):263-269. doi: 10.7861/clinmedicine.17-3-263. PMID: 28572229; PMCID: PMC6297575.
